title: Activate the Government Accelerator Pack
description: Install the ready-made configuration so the platform is useful on day one.
text: Activate the Government Accelerator Pack !The Packages screen — install ready made configuration packs, or export/import this tenant's configuration. Steps 1. Packages → install gov starter . It installs nine packages in dependency order: system config, gov roles, model registry, governance baseline, routing baseline, prompt library, agent library, integration templates, and default dashboards. 2. (Optional) install gov accelerators for 8 ready workflows, and a sector pack: package ministry , package municipality , package university , or package healthcare . 3. Configure model credentials and enable the models you'll use (Configure Models). 4. Activate any agents you need, and complete any integration connectors. 10 government roles, 11 (disabled) models across 10 providers, 9 enabled guardrails, a routing policy + 5 rules, 8 published prompts, 7 (disabled) agents + 3 tools, 10 integration templates, 6 dashboards, and bilingual (en/ar) system config. ::: Models, agents, and integrations install disabled/template on purpose — add credentials and switch on only what you need, so nothing runs (or bills) before it's configured. ::: Packages are idempotent (safe to re apply) and everything they create is a normal, editable record . See Default Configurations for the full inventory.

title: Configure Data Sources
description: Create a knowledge collection and load documents so the AI can answer from your content.
text: Configure Data Sources !The Data & Knowledge screen — collections, document ingestion, and semantic search. You need an enabled embedding model (modality embedding , e.g. text embedding 3 small ). Add it under Configure Models first. ::: Steps 1. Knowledge → create a Collection — give it a key/name and choose the embedding model . 2. Add documents — either paste text or upload files ( .txt , .md , .pdf , .json ). Each document is chunked, embedded, and stored. 3. Verify — use the Search tester to run a query and confirm relevant chunks come back with scores. 4. Use it — send knowledge: { collection, top k } on an inference request, or build a knowledge search agent. Keep documents focused and well structured — clean source text produces better retrieval and more accurate, citable answers. ::: See Knowledge / RAG for how retrieval works end to end.

title: Configure Integrations
description: Connect external systems and register signed webhooks for platform events.
text: Configure Integrations !The Integrations screen — connectors, webhooks with delivery history, and the API catalog. Add an integration (connector) 1. Integrations → Add integration — choose a type (email / sms / http / crm / erp / …), set the config and any secret. 2. Click Test to check connectivity. The HTTP connector performs a real request. The email/SMS/e sign/CRM/ERP connectors currently validate config but do not yet send — see the roadmap. ::: Add a webhook (outbound events) 1. Integrations → Webhooks → Add webhook — enter the URL and the events to subscribe to (e.g. inference.completed , inference.failed , inference.blocked ). 2. Copy the signing secret now — it is shown only once. You'll use it to verify deliveries (HMAC SHA256 over the raw body, header X Veevra Signature ). 3. Test the webhook and check Deliveries for the result. Always verify the X Veevra Signature on your receiving endpoint before trusting a payload, and return a 2xx quickly — failures are retried with backoff. ::: See Webhooks for verification details and the event payload.

title: Configure Models
description: Add a provider, store its credential, register a model, test it, and enable it.
text: Configure Models !The Models screen — register models, run a live test, and enable them. Steps 1. Add a provider — Providers → Add provider → choose type ( openai , anthropic , azure openai , onprem , generic ), name it, set the base URL if needed → save. 2. Add a credential — on that provider, open Credentials → paste the API key → save. It is stored encrypted and shown only once. 3. Register the model — Models → Add model (wizard): Provider + routing key (e.g. openai gpt 4o ) → Identity (display name + provider model name gpt 4o ) → Configuration (modality, credential, context window, on prem?) → Capabilities + tags → Review → Create. 4. Test — click Test for a live call. If it succeeds, click Enable . New models start disabled until a credential is set and you enable them — so nothing can be used (or billed) by accident. ::: Set per model pricing so cost dashboards and "cheapest" routing work, and run a health check so routing can avoid unhealthy models. ::: Next: Configure Routing Rules to direct traffic to your new model.

title: Configure Policies
description: Add governance guardrails that mask private data and block unsafe content.
text: Configure Policies !The Governance screen — guardrails, a scan tester, violations, and alerts. Steps 1. Governance → Add guardrail. 2. Choose: Direction — input / output / both. Rule type — pii , injection , content , or regex . Action — redact , block , flag , or require approval . Severity and priority . Config — e.g. PII entities ( email , phone , credit card ), a custom regex pattern, or content keywords. 3. Save — it is enabled immediately and applies to every request. 4. Use the Scan tester to paste sample text and confirm what gets detected, masked, or blocked. Install the governance baseline package first — it seeds 9 ready guardrails (PII, national ID, passport, financial/IBAN, injection, jailbreak, content) — then add organisation specific ones on top. ::: A block guardrail ends the request (status blocked ) and records a violation — still fully audited. Test new block rules with the Scan tester before enabling broadly. ::: See Policy & Governance and Default Policies.

title: Configure Prompts
description: Create, review, and publish prompt templates that the platform uses at runtime.
text: Configure Prompts !The Prompts library — templates with status and the approval/publish workflow. Steps 1. Prompts → New template — set key, name, category, and the body using {{variables}} . Declare each variable's type (string/text/number/boolean), whether it is required, and any default. 2. The template starts as a Draft . Edit and refine; use the Test panel to render it with sample values and run it against a live model. 3. Submit for approval . 4. A user with prompt.approve opens it → Approve → Publish . The published version becomes the one the AI Service Bus uses. Publishing a new version archives the previous published one. Old versions are kept, so you can compare or roll back. ::: ::: The prompt library package seeds 8 ready to use published templates — see Default Prompts.

title: Configure Routing Rules
description: Create a policy and rules that decide which model handles each request.
text: Configure Routing Rules !The Routing screen — policies, rules, fallback chains, and the simulate panel. Steps 1. Routing → New policy — name it and set a priority (higher policies evaluate first). 2. Expand the policy → Add rule (wizard): Match (name + conditions JSON, e.g. {"field":"sensitivity","op":"eq","value":"high"} ) → Action ( route to a model with fallbacks, or onprem / cheapest / fastest / chain / experiment / block ) → Review → Add. 3. Simulate — use the Simulate panel to dry run a request (set sensitivity / language / intent / tags) and confirm the rule fires as expected. No model is actually called. Common rules Goal Condition Action Keep sensitive data on prem sensitivity == high onprem Arabic to a capable model language == ar route to that model Save cost on lookups intent == knowledge search cheapest Last resort fallback {} (match all) chain (cloud fallback) Rules within a policy run by priority ; the first match wins. Put your most specific rules at the highest priority and a catch all at the lowest. ::: See Routing Rules for the full reference.

title: First-Time Setup
description: Get a fresh Veevra deployment to its first governed AI request.
text: First Time Setup The bootstrap admin is admin@veevra.io / ChangeMe!Admin123 . Change it immediately after first login (top right user menu). ::: !Once configured, the dashboard summarises health, cost, and what needs attention. Steps 1. Log in at the portal URL with the bootstrap admin and change the password. 2. Install the starter pack — go to Packages → install gov starter (roles, models, governance, routing, prompts, agents, dashboards, system config). Optionally add gov accelerators and a sector pack. 3. Add a model credential — Providers → pick a provider → add its API key (stored encrypted). 4. Enable a model — Models → Test → Enable . 5. Run your first request — AI Service Bus → Playground → type a request → Run . Watch the pipeline and the trace. Seeded models, agents, and integrations are intentionally disabled/template until you configure credentials. This prevents accidental usage and cost. ::: What's enabled vs. what you configure Enabled out of the box You must configure Roles, baseline guardrails (9), published prompts, dashboards, system config Model credentials + enable, activate agents , complete integrations , upload knowledge Next: Configure Models · Activate the Accelerator Pack.

title: AI Service Bus Architecture
description: The 9-stage governed pipeline that processes every AI request.
text: AI Service Bus Architecture The AI Service Bus is a pipeline : a shared request context travels through ordered stages, each adding or checking something. It has 9 stages . The stages Stage Does 1 Intake Creates the request + trace ID; starts the trace 2 Context Gathers tenant settings, knowledge request, messages 3 Policy PRE Masks PII; blocks injection — before the model 4 Route Selects the model (rules, sensitivity, language) + fallbacks 5 Prompt Renders the published template; injects retrieved knowledge 6 Orchestrate Calls the model; retries; falls back on failure 7 Policy POST Scans/masks the output 8 Respond Computes cost/tokens/latency; shapes the answer 9 Audit Writes the hash chained trace — always runs Even if a request is blocked at governance or the model fails , stage 9 still records a complete, tamper evident trace. Nothing escapes the audit. ::: A full deep dive (inputs/outputs/examples per component) is on the AI Service Bus module page.

title: Database Architecture
description: How data is organised — tenancy, soft-deletes, and the key entity relationships.
text: Database Architecture The platform uses PostgreSQL 16 with the pgvector extension (for knowledge embeddings). There are ~59 tables across the modules. Conventions UUID primary keys ( id ). Timestamps created at / updated at , and soft delete ( deleted at ) on most tables — rows are marked deleted, not erased, preserving history. Tenant scoping — most tables carry tenant id (FK → tenants ), so each organisation's data is isolated. JSONB columns hold flexible config/detail; secrets are stored encrypted (AES 256 GCM). Key relationships Notable tables by area Identity: tenants , users , memberships , roles , permissions , api keys , audit logs (hash chained). Models: providers , credentials (encrypted), models , model health , model pricing . Requests: inference requests , request trace stages , usage records , model calls . Knowledge: collections , documents , chunks (vector embedding), retrieval logs . Agents: agents , agent runs , workflows , workflow runs , approval tasks . A full entity relationship diagram (all 59 tables) with per table columns is maintained in the engineering documentation set ( docs/platform guide ). A developer facing database reference page will be added under the Developer Guide. :::

title: Deployment Architecture
description: How the platform is packaged and runs in development and production.
text: Deployment Architecture The platform runs as a small set of cooperating services. Components Service Role web Next.js portal (this UI) api FastAPI backend — API, AI Service Bus, all modules worker Celery — background jobs (health checks, webhook delivery, scheduled workflows, alert evaluation) beat Celery scheduler — triggers periodic jobs migrate Runs alembic upgrade head before the API starts PostgreSQL + pgvector All data + vector embeddings for RAG Redis Cache, rate limiting, live trace pub/sub, Celery broker Environments Development: Docker Compose (6 services). Production: a Helm chart ( deploy/helm/veevra ) with a pre install migration job, API + worker + beat (with autoscaling), web, ingress, and secrets — or docker compose.prod.yml . See the Operations → Deployment guide for commands and the Environment Variables reference.

title: High-Level Architecture
description: The layers of the platform and how they fit together.
text: High Level Architecture Veevra is built in clear layers: a web portal , a backend that runs the AI Service Bus and all modules, the AI models (cloud + on prem), and the data layer (PostgreSQL + Redis). The layers Layer What it does Why it exists Frontend portal (Next.js) Screens to configure & operate the platform A safe, role aware UI without touching code Backend (FastAPI) API, security, the Service Bus, all modules Keeps security & governance consistent AI Service Bus The 9 stage governed pipeline No request can skip identity, routing, governance, audit Model Registry Catalogue of models, capabilities, pricing, health, encrypted credentials Routing needs to know what exists and what it costs Routing Engine Picks the model by rules Sensitive data stays on prem; cost control Prompt Manager Versioned, approved prompt templates Prompts are IP + a compliance surface Governance Engine Masks PII, blocks injection, filters content Privacy & compliance are non negotiable Knowledge / RAG Answers from your own documents Grounded answers with sources Agents Multi step, tool using assistants Complex tasks need more than one call Audit Tamper evident record Compliance & trust Database PostgreSQL + pgvector + Redis Durable storage + fast coordination Every layer connects through the AI Service Bus. The next page traces a request end to end. :::

title: End-to-End Request Flow
description: What happens, step by step, when an AI request enters the platform.
text: End to End Request Flow Every AI request — whether from the portal or an external system — follows the same governed path. The steps 1. Submit — a user (portal) or system (API key) sends a request to POST /api/v1/inference . 2. Authenticate & authorise — the session/API key is verified and the inference.run permission checked. 3. Intake — the AI Service Bus creates a request record + trace id and starts the live trace. 4. Context & intent — tenant settings and any knowledge request are assembled; the intent is normalised. 5. Policy (pre) — PII/national ID is masked and injection is blocked before the model sees anything. 6. Route — routing rules pick the model (sensitivity, language, intent, cost) + fallbacks. 7. Prompt — the published template is rendered with variables; retrieved knowledge is injected. 8. Orchestrate — the model is called, with retry and fallback on failure. 9. Policy (post) — the model's output is scanned/masked. 10. Respond — cost, tokens, and latency are computed; the answer is shaped. 11. Audit — a hash chained trace is written ( always ), and an event is emitted. Because the path is identical for everyone, no request can skip identity checks, governance, routing, or auditing. See a concrete walk through in the Citizen Complaint Flow. :::

title: AI Agents
description: Autonomous, tool-using assistants and multi-step workflows with human-in-the-loop approval.
text: AI Agents !The Agents screen — agents, tools, workflows, and pending approvals. How It Works What it does — Defines agents (system prompt + model + tools + memory) and multi step workflows, with optional human approval steps. Why it matters — Some tasks need more than one model call — e.g. classify a complaint, look up a case, then draft a reply. How it connects — Each agent turn runs through the Service Bus , so governance, routing, and audit still apply. Tools can call Knowledge (retrieval) or external systems (HTTP). Example flow — A Document Processing Agent validates an upload, looks up the case, lists missing items, and pauses for human approval before finalising. See Document Processing Flow. Related configuration screens — Agents (agents, tools, workflows, approvals). Concepts Tool types: retrieval (knowledge search), http (call an API), echo . Loop: the model returns a tool call or a final answer (bounded by max steps ). Memory: optional conversation recall across turns. Human in the loop: a step can require approval; the run pauses until an authorised user approves. Key APIs /agents , /agents/tools , /agents/workflows (+run/runs), /agents/{id}/run , /agents/approvals/{id}/approve reject . Database tables tools , agents , agent tool , agent runs , agent memory , workflows , workflow runs , workflow step runs , approval tasks .

title: AI Service Bus
description: The flagship module — run governed AI requests and watch them flow in real time.
text: AI Service Bus This is where AI requests actually run. You can submit a request in the Playground and watch it travel through the pipeline (Request → Routing → Prompt → Model → Agent → Response) on an animated, live screen — and inspect any past request in full detail. ::: !The AI Service Bus console — an animated pipeline, a request Playground, and a live trace feed. How It Works What it does — Submits a governed AI request and processes it through the 9 stage pipeline, recording a complete trace. Why it matters — It guarantees no AI request can skip identity checks, routing, governance, or auditing. It is the single, consistent path for all inference. How it connects — Orchestrates Routing, Prompts, Governance, Knowledge, and the Model Registry; writes to Audit Logs. Example flow — See the Citizen Complaint Flow for a full walk through. Related configuration screens — Routing Rules, Prompts, Governance, Models. Main screens AI Service Bus console — animated pipeline + a Playground to build/run a request + a live trace feed (WebSocket). Requests — searchable table of every request with a stage timeline side panel; a detail page with Replay . Key APIs Database tables inference requests , request trace stages , usage records , model calls . The bus's behaviour is driven by the other modules — routing rules, published prompts, and enabled guardrails — so configuring those configures the bus. :::

title: Audit Logs
description: A tamper-evident, append-only record of everything that happened.
text: Audit Logs !The Audit Log — filterable entries with a detail panel and a chain verify button. How It Works What it does — Records every significant action (and a full per request trace), with each entry hash chained to the previous one. Why it matters — Compliance, investigations, and trust. Hash chaining makes tampering detectable. How it connects — Every module writes audit entries on create/update/delete; the Service Bus writes the per request trace; the Verify action recomputes the chain. Example flow — An auditor filters to model.write actions, opens one, and clicks Verify to prove the log was not altered. Related configuration screens — Audit Log (filterable table + detail panel + Verify). Integrity Each entry stores a seq , its own hash , and the prev hash . Because each hash includes the previous entry's hash, altering any record breaks the chain — surfaced by GET /audit logs/verify . Key APIs /audit logs (list), /audit logs/verify (integrity check). Database tables audit logs (append only; prev hash / hash / seq ).

title: Dashboard
description: The executive control centre — health, activity, cost, and what needs attention.
text: Dashboard !The executive control centre — health/governance/compliance scores, KPIs, charts, and a priority feed. How It Works What it does — Aggregates live metrics, cost, governance, and health into hero scores, KPI cards, charts, and a "needs attention" feed. Why it matters — Leaders and operators get an instant, trustworthy read on AI usage and risk without digging through screens. How it connects — Reads from Observability (metrics/cost/health), Governance (scores/alerts), and the Service Bus (live activity). Example flow — Each morning a director opens the dashboard, sees 2 open PII block alerts in the feed, and clicks through to Governance to review. Related configuration screens — Dashboards (configurable widgets), Observability, Governance. Main screens Dashboard — hero scores (AI Health, Governance, Compliance), six KPI cards, charts (requests & errors, cost by model, routing mix), priority feed, live Service Bus ticker. Dashboards — configurable, widget based dashboards seeded per use case. Key APIs /metrics/overview , /metrics/timeseries , /metrics/by model , /costs/summary , /system/health , /governance/report ; configurable dashboards via /dashboards . Database tables dashboards (saved widget layouts); reads aggregate from inference requests , usage records , policy violations , alerts .

title: Data Sources
description: Where the knowledge base's documents come from.
text: Data Sources !The Data & Knowledge screen — collections backed by data sources. How It Works What it does — Registers where documents originate (file uploads today; connector types for S3/API/database are extensible). Why it matters — Documents are linked to their source for traceability and future automated sync. How it connects — Documents in a Knowledge collection reference their data source; future automated sync would use Integrations. Example flow — A "Demo Policies" upload source holds the seeded policy documents in the demo tenant. Related configuration screens — Knowledge (data sources back the documents). Key APIs /knowledge/sources (list / create / delete). Database tables data sources (type, config, status, last sync at). Today the upload source type is implemented; automated connectors (S3, external APIs, databases) are on the roadmap. :::

title: Integrations
description: Connect the platform to your other systems — inbound and outbound.
text: Integrations !The Integrations screen — connectors, webhooks with delivery history, and an API catalog. How It Works What it does — Manages outbound webhooks (signed, retried event deliveries), integration connectors (email/SMS/HTTP/CRM/ERP…), and an API catalog . Why it matters — The platform must fit into the organisation's existing ecosystem — receiving requests and pushing results to downstream systems. How it connects — The Service Bus emits events ( inference.completed/failed/blocked ) from its Audit stage → fanned out to subscribed webhooks; Agents use HTTP tools. Example flow — Register a webhook for inference.completed ; every governed answer is pushed (HMAC signed) so the case management record updates automatically. Related configuration screens — Integrations (connectors, webhooks, deliveries, API catalog). The HTTP connector and webhooks perform real requests. The email/SMS/e sign/CRM/ERP connectors currently validate configuration but do not yet send — real delivery is on the roadmap. ::: Key APIs /integrations (+test), /webhooks (+test, +deliveries), /events/emit , /catalog . Database tables integrations (encrypted secret), webhooks (encrypted signing secret), webhook deliveries .

title: Knowledge / RAG
description: Answer using your own documents, with source references (Retrieval-Augmented Generation).
text: Knowledge / RAG !The Knowledge screen — collections, documents, and a semantic search tester. How It Works What it does — Lets you upload documents, which are chunked, embedded (turned into meaning vectors), and stored; at query time the most relevant chunks are retrieved and injected into the prompt. Why it matters — Answers are grounded in your policies and documents, with citations — essential for trustworthy government use. How it connects — Uses an embedding model from the Model Registry; the Service Bus injects retrieved context; Agents use a knowledge search tool. Example flow — Upload the "Citizen Service Charter"; when a complaint is processed, the relevant charter section is retrieved and cited. See Knowledge Search Flow. Related configuration screens — Knowledge (collections, documents, search). Pipeline Key APIs /knowledge/collections , /knowledge/collections/{id}/documents , /knowledge/collections/{id}/upload , /knowledge/search . Database tables collections , documents , chunks (pgvector embedding), ingestion jobs , retrieval logs .

title: Model Registry
description: The catalogue of AI providers and models, with capabilities, pricing, health, and encrypted credentials.
text: Model Registry Before the platform can use an AI model, you register it here: which provider it belongs to, what it can do, what it costs, and its (encrypted) API key. Models start disabled until you add a credential and switch them on — so nothing is used by accident. ::: !The Models screen — registered models with live health indicators, enable/disable, and test. How It Works What it does — Catalogues providers and models with capabilities, pricing, health, and encrypted credentials. Why it matters — Routing must know which models exist, what they can do, what they cost, and whether they're healthy — to choose wisely and control spend. How it connects — Routing selects from enabled models; the Service Bus calls them via adapters; Observability tracks health & cost. Example flow — Add an Azure OpenAI provider → store its key (encrypted) → register azure gpt 4o → Test → Enable . It is now eligible for routing. Related configuration screens — Providers, Models, Routing. Model categories Category Examples Why Cloud OpenAI GPT 4o, Anthropic Claude, Azure OpenAI Most capable; no infrastructure On prem Llama, Mistral, Mixtral Data never leaves the data centre Specialised OCR, Embeddings, Speech to Text, Translation Purpose built; combined with chat models Key APIs Database tables providers , credentials (encrypted), models , capabilities , model capability , model health , model pricing . API keys are stored encrypted (AES 256 GCM) and are never shown again after creation. Keep them in your own secrets store as well. :::

title: Policy & Governance
description: Privacy, safety, and compliance enforced on every request — masking, injection blocking, content policy.
text: Policy & Governance !The Governance screen — guardrails, a scan tester, violations, and alerts. How It Works What it does — Applies guardrails that detect and act on input/output: mask PII / national ID / passport / financial data, block prompt injection and jailbreaks, filter content. Records violations and raises alerts. Why it matters — Privacy, safety, and regulatory compliance are non negotiable in government and enterprise. How it connects — The Service Bus Policy pre and Policy post stages call it; the Dashboard shows the governance score; alerts surface in Observability. Example flow — The "National ID detection" guardrail ( \b\d{10}\b , redact) masks IDs before any model sees them. Related configuration screens — Governance (guardrails, scan tester, violations, alerts). Guardrails Direction: input / output / both. Rule type: pii / injection / content / regex. Action: allow / redact / block / flag / require approval. Each carries a severity and priority . Guardrails apply both before the model (sanitise input) and after (scan output), so private data is protected on the way in and on the way out. ::: Key APIs /governance/guardrails , /governance/scan , /governance/violations , /governance/alerts (+ack/resolve), /governance/report . Database tables guardrails , policy violations , alerts , compliance policies .

title: Prompt Manager
description: A governed library of versioned, approved prompt templates with variables.
text: Prompt Manager !The Prompts library — templates with status and an approval/publish workflow. How It Works What it does — Stores prompt templates with typed {{variables}} , versions every change, and runs an approve/publish workflow. Why it matters — Prompts are valuable IP and a compliance surface; they must be reviewed and version controlled, not buried in code. How it connects — The Service Bus Prompt stage renders the published version with the request's variables; Agents and workflows reference templates. Example flow — A team drafts a "Correspondence Drafting" template, submits for approval; a Governance Officer approves and publishes; the bus now uses it. Related configuration screens — Prompts (library + version editor + test). Lifecycle draft → in review → approved → published . Only published versions are used at runtime, so unreviewed changes can never reach production. Key APIs /prompts , /prompts/{id}/versions , workflow submit approve reject publish , render , test . Database tables prompt templates , prompt versions , prompt approvals , prompt evals .

title: Routing Rules
description: Decide which model handles each request — by sensitivity, language, intent, cost, and availability.
text: Routing Rules !The Routing screen — policies, rules, fallback chains, experiments, and a simulate panel. How It Works What it does — Chooses the model for each request using prioritised rules (conditions → action), with fallback chains and A/B experiments. Why it matters — Different jobs need different models; sensitive data must stay on prem; cost and latency must be controlled — all without code changes. How it connects — Selects from enabled models in the Model Registry; invoked by the Service Bus Route stage. Example flow — "If sensitivity = high → on prem model" (priority 300); "If language = ar → Arabic capable model" (200); else cloud fallback chain. Related configuration screens — Routing (policies, rules, chains, experiments, simulate), Models. Conditions & actions Conditions: field (sensitivity, language, intent, source, max tokens, tags, model key) + op (eq, in, gt, contains…) + value . {} = match all. Actions: route (specific model + fallbacks), cheapest , fastest , onprem , experiment , chain , block . Use the Simulate panel to dry run a request (sensitivity/language/intent) and see exactly which model would be chosen — no model is actually called. ::: Key APIs /routing/policies , /routing/rules , /routing/chains , /routing/experiments (+start/stop), /routing/simulate . Database tables routing policies , routing rules , fallback chains , experiments .

title: Settings
description: Platform-wide configuration — languages, regions, notifications, settings, and departments.
text: Settings !The System Config screen — tabbed Languages, Regions, Notifications, Settings, and Departments. How It Works What it does — Manages languages, regions/time zones, notification templates, generic key value settings, and the organisational department hierarchy. Why it matters — Localisation (English + Arabic right to left), branding, security defaults, and department structure shape the experience for every user. How it connects — Drives portal localisation/branding, notifications, and department routing used by agents/workflows. Example flow — Set platform.default language = ar and add the Saudi Arabia region (Asia/Riyadh) so the portal shows Arabic, right to left, in local time. Related configuration screens — System Config (tabbed: Languages, Regions, Notifications, Settings, Departments). What you can configure Area Examples Languages en (default), ar (RTL); add more Regions IANA time zones (e.g. Asia/Riyadh), locales Notification templates per channel (email/SMS/in app) and locale Settings branding (platform name), localization, security (session timeout, MFA, password length), AI defaults, environment tier Departments hierarchical org units (code, parent) Key APIs /system config/{languages regions notification templates settings departments} . Database tables languages , regions , notification templates , settings , departments .

title: Users, Roles & Tenants
description: Identity and access — who can log in, what they can do, and which organisation they belong to.
text: Users, Roles & Tenants !The Users screen — filterable list with role/status/MFA and a detail panel. How It Works What it does — Manages users, role based permissions, multi tenancy, API keys, MFA, and SSO/OIDC. Why it matters — Only the right people/systems may perform each action, and everything is attributable. How it connects — Every module is gated by a permission; all changes are written to the Audit Log. Example flow — An admin creates a "Governance Officer" user, assigns the role, and they immediately see only governance/audit screens. Related configuration screens — Users, Roles, Tenants, API Keys, SSO providers. Concepts Tenant — one organisation/department; data is isolated per tenant. Membership — links a user to a tenant with a role. Role — a bundle of permissions (system roles: Owner/Admin/Operator/Developer/Auditor/Viewer; plus custom). API key — a scoped secret for machine access. MFA / SSO — TOTP second factor; OpenID Connect single sign on with group→role mapping. Key APIs /auth/login refresh logout , /me , /users , /roles , /permissions , /tenants , /api keys , /oidc providers . Database tables tenants , users , memberships , roles , permissions , role permission , api keys , refresh tokens , oidc providers .

title: API Endpoints
description: The endpoint families, with the permission each requires, plus where to find the live reference.
text: API Endpoints All endpoints are under /api/v1 . Each requires a permission (see Authentication). !Every request the API serves is fully traced and inspectable under Requests. Endpoint families Area Examples Permission Auth & IAM /auth/login , /me , /users , /roles , /tenants , /api keys , /audit logs varies Model Registry /providers , /models (+ /enable , /disable , /test ), /capabilities model. AI Service Bus /inference , /inference/stream , /requests , /ws/traces inference.run , request.read Routing /routing/policies , /routing/rules , /routing/chains , /routing/experiments , /routing/simulate routing. Prompts /prompts , /prompts/{id}/versions , …/publish prompt. Governance /governance/guardrails , /governance/scan , /governance/violations , /governance/alerts , /governance/report governance. Knowledge /knowledge/collections , …/documents , …/upload , /knowledge/search knowledge. Agents /agents , /agents/tools , /agents/workflows , …/run , /agents/approvals/{id}/approve agent. Observability /metrics/ , /costs/summary , /sla targets , /alert rules , /system/health observability. Integration /integrations , /webhooks , /events/emit , /catalog integration. Config /dashboards , /system config/ , /seed/ dashboard. , config. The inference call Returns trace id , output , selected model key , usage (tokens + cost), latency ms , and a policy summary of what was masked. GET /api/v1/catalog returns every endpoint (path, methods, tags). The interactive OpenAPI docs are served by the backend at /docs . :::

title: API Overview
description: How to call the Veevra API, authentication, and where to find every endpoint.
text: API Overview The whole platform is a REST API. The web portal is just a friendly front end over it. External systems can do anything the portal can, by calling /api/v1/... with an API key. ::: Basics Base URL: all endpoints are under /api/v1 . Health ( /health ) and the root ( / ) are unversioned. Auth: browsers use a JWT in an httpOnly cookie (from POST /auth/login ); machines send an API key header. Each endpoint requires a permission (e.g. inference.run ). Responses: JSON; 204 = success with no body. Errors return a structured error object. Run a governed request Response includes trace id , output , selected model key , usage (tokens + cost), latency ms , and a policy summary (what was masked). !Every request is fully traced — searchable, with a stage by stage timeline. Discover endpoints GET /api/v1/catalog returns every endpoint (path, methods, tags) — a built in developer portal. FastAPI's interactive docs are at /docs . See Authentication for tokens and API keys, and API Endpoints for the full reference. :::

title: Authentication
description: How browsers and machines authenticate, and how permissions are enforced.
text: Authentication Two ways to authenticate Caller Method Browser / portal POST /api/v1/auth/login → sets a JWT in an httpOnly cookie ( veevra access ) plus a rotating refresh cookie. Optional TOTP MFA and SSO/OIDC . Machine / external system An API key sent as a header. Keys are scoped to specific permissions and can expire/be revoked. Login On success the response returns the session (user, active tenant, permissions) and sets the auth cookies. The access token is refreshed transparently via POST /api/v1/auth/refresh . Authorization (RBAC) Every endpoint requires a permission code (e.g. inference.run , model.write , governance.read ). Permissions are bundled into roles ; users get roles per tenant. An API key carries its own scopes. If the caller lacks the permission, the request is rejected (and the attempt is audited). MFA & SSO MFA: POST /api/v1/auth/mfa/setup enable disable (TOTP authenticator app). SSO (OIDC): GET /api/v1/auth/oidc/{slug}/login → provider → /callback ; supports PKCE, JWKS verification, and group→role mapping. For server to server calls, create a scoped API key (Admin → API Keys) with only the permissions it needs, and set an expiry. ::: The login secret is returned only in the session; tokens live in httpOnly cookies (not readable by scripts). Always call the API over HTTPS in production ( COOKIE SECURE=true ). :::

title: Event Bus
description: How platform events are emitted and fanned out to subscribers.
text: Event Bus How it works 1. The AI Service Bus emits a named event from its Audit stage (e.g. inference.completed ) — best effort, never blocking the request. 2. The Event service finds every webhook subscribed to that event. 3. A background Celery worker delivers each one (signed, with retry/backoff) and records the outcome. Events Event When inference.completed A request finished successfully inference.failed A request errored inference.blocked Governance blocked a request Emitting manually POST /api/v1/events/emit raises an event into the bus (for testing or custom flows). Subscribers are matched and delivered the same way. Delivery runs asynchronously via Celery (Redis broker), so emitting an event never slows down the inference request. Consumers should be idempotent — see Webhooks. :::

title: Integration Development
description: Patterns for connecting your systems to the platform — inbound and outbound.
text: Integration Development Three patterns 1. Inbound — your system calls the platform Use an API key (scoped) to call /api/v1/inference (and any other endpoint). The full governed pipeline applies. Best for: "summarise this", "classify that" from a case system or portal. 2. Outbound — the platform notifies your system Register a webhook for inference.completed (etc.); the platform pushes a signed payload when work finishes. Best for: updating a downstream record asynchronously. See Webhooks. 3. Agent driven — the platform calls your system mid task Give an agent an http tool pointing at your endpoint. During a run, the agent can look up a case, validate data, or trigger an action in your system, then continue reasoning. Mark the tool requires approval for human in the loop control. Connectors For email/SMS/CRM/ERP/DMS, register an integration of the matching type (Admin → Integrations). The HTTP connector is live; others currently validate config (real delivery on the roadmap). New connector types are added behind the connector interface. Scope API keys minimally, verify webhook signatures, and make outbound handlers idempotent. For agent tools that change state, require approval. :::

title: Model Adapter Development
description: How model adapters work and how to add support for a new provider.
text: Model Adapter Development What an adapter is Each provider's API differs (OpenAI vs. Anthropic vs. an on prem server). A Model Adapter implements a shared interface so the AI Service Bus can call any model uniformly. The orchestrate stage looks up the model's provider, decrypts its credential in memory, and invokes the matching adapter. Built in adapters Adapter Notes openai Chat completions + embeddings anthropic Messages API azure openai Deployment based chat + embeddings onprem OpenAI compatible; requires an explicit base URL generic Configurable HTTP paths, auth scheme, and response parsing (dot notation) — covers most custom/HTTP engines Adding a new provider — two paths 1. Configuration only (no code): register a provider of type generic and point it at the engine's HTTP endpoint, setting the request/response paths in config. This covers many OCR/STT/translation/HTTP models today. 2. A native adapter (code): implement the adapter interface ( complete() , optional embed() , health() ), register it in the adapter factory by provider type, and it becomes available to the registry. Use an injectable HTTP client so it is unit testable with a mock transport. Prefer the generic adapter for HTTP style engines; write a native adapter only when the provider needs bespoke auth, streaming, or response handling. ::: Specialised native adapters (OCR, speech to text, translation) and true token by token streaming via an adapter stream() seam are on the roadmap. :::

title: Webhooks
description: Receive signed, retried event deliveries when AI requests complete.
text: Webhooks Register Create a webhook (Admin → Integrations → Webhooks, or POST /api/v1/webhooks ) with a URL and the events to subscribe to. Copy the signing secret shown once. Receive & verify The platform sends a POST to your URL with headers: X Veevra Event — the event name (e.g. inference.completed ) X Veevra Delivery — a unique delivery ID X Veevra Signature — HMAC SHA256 of the raw body, keyed with your signing secret Verify by recomputing the HMAC over the raw request body and comparing (constant time) to the header: Delivery & retries Return 2xx quickly to acknowledge. Non 2xx or timeouts are retried with exponential backoff (up to 5 attempts). Inspect outcomes under Webhooks → Deliveries (or GET /api/v1/webhooks/{id}/deliveries ). Always verify the signature before trusting a payload, and treat deliveries as at least once — make your handler idempotent using X Veevra Delivery . ::: Events available: inference.completed , inference.failed , inference.blocked . See the Event Bus.

title: Business License Flow
description: A worked example — validate a business-license application and produce an eligibility decision.
text: Business License Flow An applicant submits a business licence application. The platform extracts the key details, checks them against the requirements, and recommends approve / reject / needs more info — with a licensing officer giving final approval. ::: The workflow 1. Extract — an extraction prompt reads the application and returns structured fields (business type, owner, documents provided) as JSON. 2. Decide — a License Officer agent compares submitted vs. required documents (grounded in the regulations knowledge base) and proposes a decision with reasons. 3. Human approval — the step is marked requires approval ; the run pauses and creates an approval task for the licensing officer. 4. Finalise — on approval, the workflow drafts the applicant notification; on rejection, it records the reason. Each model turn runs through the AI Service Bus, so PII is masked, routing applies, and the whole run is audited. The decision is recommended by AI but made by a human. ::: This is seeded as the business license workflow in the demo content. See AI Agents.

title: Citizen Complaint Flow
description: A worked end-to-end example — summarise a complaint and recommend a department, with privacy enforced.
text: Citizen Complaint Flow A government employee, Sara , asks: "Summarise this citizen complaint and recommend which department should handle it." The complaint text includes the citizen's national ID and phone number. ::: !The request flows live through the AI Service Bus pipeline, stage by stage. What happens, step by step 1. Submit — Sara uses the Service Bus Playground (or an external system calls POST /inference ). 2. Authenticate — her session and inference.run permission are verified. 3. Intake — a request record + trace id are created; the live trace feed lights up. 4. Policy PRE — the national ID ( \b\d{10}\b ) and phone are redacted ; an injection check passes. The model never sees the raw personal data. 5. Route — routing rules pick an approved model (on prem if sensitivity were high). 6. Prompt — the published classification template is filled with the masked text + the valid department list; the Citizen Service Charter excerpt is retrieved (RAG) and injected. 7. Orchestrate — the model is called (with retry/fallback). It returns a summary and "Citizen Services." 8. Policy POST — the output is scanned; nothing leaks. 9. Respond — cost/tokens/latency computed. 10. Audit — the full, hash chained trace is written; an inference.completed event fires. Sequence Sara didn't have to think about which model to use, whether the ID would leak, or how to prove what happened later. The platform handled all of it — safe, governed, auditable AI by default . :::

title: Document Processing Flow
description: A worked example — an agent validates and classifies an uploaded document.
text: Document Processing Flow An employee uploads a submitted document. The Document Processing Agent reads it, classifies it, checks it against requirements, and lists any missing items — pausing for a human to confirm. ::: !The Document Processing Agent and its tools in the Agent Studio. Steps 1. Read/extract — the document is read (OCR/extraction) into text and structured fields. 2. Classify — the agent classifies the document type and validates required fields. 3. Look up — a case lookup tool fetches the related case for context. 4. Report — the agent lists missing items and a clear recommendation. 5. Human in the loop — the decision step requires approval before it is finalised. Because every agent turn is a governed inference, any personal data in the document is masked before the model sees it, and the full multi step run is audited . The human approves the final action. ::: See AI Agents for how tools and approvals work.

title: Knowledge Search Flow
description: A worked example — answer a question grounded in your own documents, with sources.
text: Knowledge Search Flow An employee asks: "What is the acknowledgement time for a citizen complaint?" The platform retrieves the relevant section of the Citizen Service Charter and answers from it — with a source reference. ::: !The Knowledge screen — collections, documents, and the semantic search tester. Steps 1. Embed the question — the question is turned into a meaning vector using the collection's embedding model. 2. Retrieve — the vector database (pgvector) returns the top K most similar chunks from your documents. 3. Ground the prompt — those chunks are injected into the prompt as context: "Answer only from this context; cite sources." 4. Answer — the model responds using the retrieved context, and the answer carries the source document + relevance score. The Knowledge Agent is instructed to answer only from retrieved context and to say "I don't know" if the answer isn't present — preventing confident but wrong answers and keeping responses traceable to source documents. ::: Set it up via Configure Data Sources; see Knowledge / RAG for the full mechanism.

title: Glossary
description: Plain-language definitions of every key term.
text: Glossary Term In plain words LLM (Large Language Model) The AI "brain" that understands and generates text (GPT 4o, Claude, Llama). AI Service Bus Veevra's governed pipeline every AI request passes through. Model Adapter Software that knows how to talk to a specific provider's API. Prompt The instruction you send to a model. Prompt template A reusable prompt with {{variables}} ; versioned and approved. Token The unit models read/write in (~a word piece); pricing and limits use tokens. RAG Retrieval Augmented Generation: retrieve your documents, then answer with sources. Embedding A list of numbers representing the meaning of text. Vector database Stores embeddings and finds the most similar ones (here: PostgreSQL + pgvector). Agent An AI that takes multiple steps and uses tools. Tool A capability an agent can invoke (search, HTTP call). Human in the loop A point where a person must approve before continuing. Guardrail An automated safety/privacy check on AI input/output. Policy / Governance Engine Runs the guardrails before and after the model call. PII Personally Identifiable Information (national ID, email, phone, financial). Prompt injection An attack that tries to hijack the model's instructions. Routing rule Decides which model handles a request. Fallback chain An ordered list of backup models. Audit log Tamper evident, append only record (hash chained). Tenant One organisation/department; data isolated per tenant. API key A secret token letting an external system call the API, with scopes. Webhook A URL Veevra calls to notify your system of events (signed). RBAC Role Based Access Control: permissions → roles → users. JWT / MFA / SSO (OIDC) Login token / second factor code / single sign on. Inference A single run of an AI model on a request. Trace / trace ID The full step by step record of one request. Capability / Modality What a model can do / the kind of input output (chat, vision, embedding). On prem A model running on the organisation's own servers. Sensitivity A low/medium/high label that drives stricter routing & governance.

title: Key Concepts
description: The core ideas you need to understand the platform.
text: Key Concepts Concept In plain words AI Service Bus The governed "assembly line" every AI request passes through. Model A specific AI engine (e.g. GPT 4o, Claude, on prem Llama) the platform can call. Provider The vendor/endpoint hosting models; a credential is its (encrypted) API key. Prompt template A reusable, versioned, approved instruction with {{variables}} . Routing rule An if this then that rule choosing which model handles a request. Guardrail An automated privacy/safety check (mask PII, block injection). Agent An AI assistant that takes multiple steps and uses tools. RAG Answering using your documents, with sources, via a vector search. Tenant One organisation/department; data is isolated per tenant. Audit log A tamper evident record of everything that happened. Don't memorise these — each has its own page. This table is your map; follow the links as you go. ::: The full alphabetical list is in the Glossary.

title: Platform Overview
description: What the Veevra AI Control Platform is, in plain language, and who it is for.
text: Platform Overview Veevra is an AI Control Platform — think of it as an air traffic control tower for Artificial Intelligence . Modern AI models (the technology behind tools like ChatGPT and Claude) can summarise documents, answer questions, classify paperwork, and draft letters. But a government or large enterprise can't let staff paste sensitive data into a public website. Veevra is the single, governed gateway that all AI usage flows through — so the organisation stays in control. ::: !The Veevra executive dashboard — AI health, governance and compliance scores, KPIs, and a "what needs attention" feed. What the platform does Veevra sits between your people/systems and the AI models. Every AI request passes through Veevra's AI Service Bus , which: 1. Checks who is asking and whether they are allowed. 2. Decides which AI model should answer (sensitive data can stay on private, on premises models). 3. Builds the exact instructions ( prompt ) sent to the model from approved templates. 4. Applies governance — masks national IDs, emails, phone numbers; blocks prompt injection. 5. Calls the model, retries/falls back on failure, and validates the answer. 6. Records a complete, tamper evident audit trail . Who it is for Governments, ministries, municipalities, universities, hospitals, and large enterprises — any organisation that wants the productivity of modern AI without losing control of security, cost, compliance, or data residency. What makes it different from a chatbot A chatbot answers questions . Veevra governs, routes, and audits how an entire organisation uses AI — across many models, with privacy and compliance built in. ::: A normal AI chatbot Veevra AI Control Platform One model, one vendor Many models (cloud + on prem) behind one governed gateway No governance of data PII masking, injection blocking, content policy on every call No model selection Rule based routing by sensitivity, language, intent, cost No audit of AI decisions Tamper evident audit trail of every request No fallback Automatic retry & fallback chains Continue to What Problem Veevra Solves or jump to the High Level Architecture.

title: What Problem Veevra Solves
description: The risks of ungoverned AI adoption and how Veevra eliminates them.
text: What Problem Veevra Solves Without a platform like Veevra, organisations that adopt AI end up with fragmented, risky usage. The problem Problem Consequence AI used directly via public tools Sensitive citizen/patient data leaves the country; no oversight Each department wires its own AI Duplicated effort, inconsistent security, no central cost view No record of what was asked Impossible to audit, investigate, or prove compliance Locked into one vendor High cost, no fallback, no data residency choice No guardrails Private data sent to models; injection attacks succeed How Veevra solves it Every request is authenticated, routed, governed, executed, and audited consistently — replacing the fragmented picture above. ::: Data residency — routing forces sensitive requests to on prem models. Privacy & compliance — built in masking of national IDs, passports, emails, phones, financial data. Auditability — hash chained, tamper evident logs. Cost control — per model pricing, token counting, dashboards; "cheapest capable model" routing. Reliability — automatic retry and fallback chains. Fast start — government accelerator packages seed roles, policies, prompts, and workflows. !The Governance screen — guardrails, a scan tester, violations, and alerts. See Policy & Governance for how privacy is enforced.

title: Default Agents
description: The agents and tools seeded by the accelerator (created disabled).
text: Default Agents The agent library package seeds 3 tools + 7 agents (agents created disabled until you activate them). !The Agent Studio — seeded agents with their model and tools, plus the tools library. Tools Tool Type Purpose knowledge search retrieval Search a knowledge collection case lookup echo Look up a case by ID (demo) http lookup http Call an external HTTP API Agents Agent Purpose Citizen Service Agent Answer citizen enquiries about services and procedures Document Processing Agent Validate and classify submitted documents Knowledge Agent Retrieval grounded Q&A over the knowledge base Research Agent Multi step research over internal + external sources Executive Assistant Agent Draft correspondence, briefings, summaries Compliance Agent Check content/requests against regulations Agents ship disabled . Enable the ones you need, and make sure their model and tools are configured. Tools that change state should be marked requires approval for human in the loop control. ::: See AI Agents for how agents and workflows run.

title: Default Configurations
description: What the government accelerator packages seed, and what ships enabled vs. disabled.
text: Default Configurations Veevra ships with installable "packages" of configuration so a ministry is productive immediately. Everything they create is a normal, editable record — not hard coded. ::: !The Packages screen — install packs by category; each shows what it creates and its installed status. The gov starter bundle installs Package Creates Default state system config 2 languages (en, ar RTL), 8 regions, 6 notification templates, 11 settings enabled gov roles 10 government roles enabled governance baseline 9 guardrails (PII, National ID \b\d{10}\b , passport, financial/IBAN, injection, jailbreak, content) enabled model registry 10 providers + 11 models models disabled routing baseline 1 fallback chain + 1 policy + 5 rules enabled prompt library 8 templates published agent library 3 tools + 7 agents agents disabled integration templates 10 connector templates template dashboards default 6 dashboards viewable Sector packages ( package ministry , municipality , university , healthcare ) add gov accelerators (8 workflows) plus sector specific prompts/dashboards/routing (e.g. healthcare forces on prem routing). Install gov starter , then add model credentials and enable the models you need. Models/agents/integrations ship off so nothing runs before you've configured it. ::: Enabled vs. configure ✅ Enabled: roles, guardrails, published prompts, languages/regions/settings, dashboards. ⚙️ Configure: model credentials + enable, activate agents, complete integrations, upload knowledge. See the Demo Tenant for a fully pre seeded evaluation environment.

title: Default Policies
description: The baseline governance guardrails seeded by the accelerator (enabled by default).
text: Default Policies The governance baseline package seeds 9 guardrails — all enabled . !The seeded guardrails in the Governance screen, enabled out of the box. Guardrail Type Direction Action Detects PII detection & redaction pii both redact email, phone, IP, SSN, credit card National ID detection pii both redact 10 digit ID ( \b\d{10}\b ) Passport detection pii both redact passport pattern Email detection pii output redact email (output only) Phone detection pii output redact phone (output only) Financial information pii both redact credit card + IBAN Prompt injection protection injection input block injection patterns Jailbreak protection content input block "jailbreak", "DAN mode", "ignore safety"… Content filtering content both flag customisable keywords These cover the common privacy and safety needs immediately. Add organisation specific guardrails (e.g. a custom regex for an internal reference number) on top — see Configure Policies. ::: How enforcement works (before and after the model) is described in Policy & Governance.

title: Default Prompts
description: The published prompt templates seeded by the accelerator.
text: Default Prompts The prompt library package seeds 8 templates — all published (ready to use immediately). Key Name Purpose summarization Summarization Summarise text to a target length classification Classification Classify text into exact categories extraction Field Extraction Extract specified fields as JSON translation Translation Translate between languages knowledge search Knowledge Search Answer a question from the knowledge base correspondence drafting Correspondence Drafting Draft formal official correspondence meeting minutes Meeting Minutes Structure minutes (attendees, decisions, actions) executive briefing Executive Briefing Write a one page executive briefing Sector packs add more — e.g. municipal permit review , student enquiry , patient correspondence . Each template declares typed variables and is version controlled. Edit them, or create new ones via Configure Prompts. :::

title: Default Roles
description: The roles seeded by the government accelerator (and the built-in system roles).
text: Default Roles !The Roles screen — system and government roles, each editable with its permission set. System roles (every tenant) Role Access Owner All permissions ( ) Admin Everything except tenant ownership transfer Operator Run & monitor (inference, agents, read access) Developer Build models, prompts, agents, integrations Auditor Read only + audit logs Viewer Read only operational access Government roles ( gov roles package) Role Purpose System Administrator Full control ( ) Platform Administrator Platform configuration & operations AI Administrator Models, routing, prompts, knowledge, agents Governance Officer Guardrails, compliance, audit, prompt approval Auditor Read only + audit Business Administrator Users and day to day operations Knowledge Manager Data sources, collections, documents Agent Manager Build & operate agents and workflows Integration Administrator Integrations, webhooks, API keys End User Submit requests; use approved agents and knowledge All roles are normal, editable records — adjust permissions or add custom roles at any time. System roles are marked is system and can't be deleted. :::

title: Default Routing Rules
description: The baseline routing policy seeded by the accelerator.
text: Default Routing Rules The routing baseline package seeds one fallback chain, one policy, and five rules. Fallback chain cloud fallback : openai gpt 4o → anthropic claude → onprem llama . Rules (priority order; first match wins) Priority Condition Action 300 sensitivity == high route to an on prem model 200 language == ar route to a multilingual model (fallback to another) 150 intent == document classification route to a classification strong model 140 intent == knowledge search cheapest enabled model 1 {} (match all) use the cloud fallback chain These are editable defaults. Tighten them for your environment — e.g. add a rule forcing all traffic on prem for a healthcare tenant (the package healthcare pack does exactly this at priority 500). ::: See Routing Rules for the full model.

title: Demo Tenant
description: A fully pre-configured tenant for evaluation — users, departments, and a sample knowledge base.
text: Demo Tenant POST /api/v1/seed/demo (superadmin) provisions a fully configured demo tenant for evaluation. What it creates Item Detail Users (5) admin@ , governance@ , agents@ , knowledge@ , user@demo.veevra.io — shared password Demo!Passw0rd1 (change after provisioning) Departments (5) Executive, IT, Governance & Compliance, Citizen Services, Knowledge Management Knowledge base demo kb collection with 3 policy documents: Citizen Service Charter, Data Privacy Policy, Correspondence Handling Procedure To make it live 1. Configure a model credential and enable the model. 2. Enable an embedding model so the demo knowledge base can be ingested/searched. 3. Activate any agents you want to demonstrate. The demo users share a known password — use the demo tenant for evaluation only, and change credentials before any real use. ::: This lets evaluators try knowledge search, agents, and workflows immediately. See Default Configurations.

title: Backup & Recovery
description: What to back up, how to restore, and the one secret to protect.
text: Backup & Recovery What to back up Item Why PostgreSQL Holds all configuration, requests, traces, audit, and knowledge embeddings — back it up regularly (e.g. nightly pg dump + WAL/PITR). MASTER ENCRYPTION KEY Decrypts all stored credentials. Store it in a secrets manager, backed up separately from the database. Uploaded documents If stored outside the DB, back up that store too. Redis Ephemeral — safe to lose; in flight jobs are re queued. No backup required. Restore 1. Provision PostgreSQL (with the vector extension) and restore the dump. 2. Restore the MASTER ENCRYPTION KEY into the environment / Vault. 3. Run alembic upgrade head (idempotent) to ensure schema is current. 4. Start the services; verify with /health/ready and the System Health screen. If the database is restored but MASTER ENCRYPTION KEY is lost, all encrypted provider credentials become unrecoverable — you would have to re enter every API key. Protect and back up the key independently. ::: Test restores periodically into a staging environment. Use POST /seed/export to snapshot a tenant's configuration to JSON as an extra, secret free config backup. :::

title: Deployment
description: Run Veevra locally and deploy it to a server.
text: Deployment Technology Next.js (portal) · FastAPI (backend) · PostgreSQL 16 + pgvector · Redis 7 · Celery worker + beat. Docker Compose for dev; Helm chart for production. Run locally Portal: http://localhost:3000 · API docs: http://localhost:8000/docs Login: admin@veevra.io / ChangeMe!Admin123 → install gov starter from Packages . Deploy to production Build production images and run docker compose.prod.yml (API runs alembic upgrade head on start; secure cookies + JSON logs on), or Use the Helm chart ( deploy/helm/veevra ): a pre install migration job, API + autoscaling, worker + beat, web, ingress, and a secret. Terminate TLS at the ingress/load balancer; point at managed PostgreSQL (with pgvector) and Redis if preferred. MASTER ENCRYPTION KEY (32 byte base64) decrypts all stored credentials. Back it up separately and securely — without it, encrypted credentials cannot be recovered. ::: See Environment Variables and Troubleshooting.

title: Environment Variables
description: The configuration the platform reads from its environment.
text: Environment Variables Variable Purpose DATABASE URL / POSTGRES PostgreSQL connection REDIS HOST / REDIS URL Redis (cache, queue, live trace pub/sub) MASTER ENCRYPTION KEY 32 byte base64 AES 256 key that encrypts credentials/secrets JWT SECRET / signing keys Sign access/refresh tokens COOKIE SECURE true in production (HTTPS only cookies) CORS ORIGINS , PORTAL URL , API INTERNAL URL , NEXT PUBLIC API BASE URL Cross origin + portal/API URLs (and the browser WebSocket base) VAULT Optional: use HashiCorp Vault as the key provider instead of an env key LOG JSON Structured JSON logs in production EMBEDDING DIM Vector dimension for knowledge embeddings (fixed at 1536) MASTER ENCRYPTION KEY decrypts every stored credential. Back it up securely and separately — without it, encrypted provider keys cannot be recovered. Generate a real 32 byte base64 value; never ship the example. ::: Provider API keys are entered per provider as credentials in the portal and stored encrypted in the database — not as global environment variables. :::

title: Logs
description: Where logs come from and how to read them.
text: Logs Application logs View with docker compose logs f api worker web (or your orchestrator's log viewer). In production, set LOG JSON=true for structured JSON logs that aggregate cleanly into ELK/Loki/CloudWatch. The development backend has verbose SQL echo (noisy but normal). What's logged where Source Contains api Requests, pipeline activity, errors worker / beat Background jobs: health checks, webhook deliveries, scheduled workflows web Next.js portal server logs Audit trail (separate from logs) For "who did what", use the Audit Log — an append only, hash chained record that is tamper evident (verifiable via /audit logs/verify ). Unlike application logs, it is a compliance artefact, not a debugging stream. Correlate a user visible issue to its internals using the request trace id — it ties together the request, its pipeline stages, usage, and the audit entry. :::

title: Monitoring
description: How to watch platform health, metrics, cost, and SLAs.
text: Monitoring !The dashboard surfaces live health, cost, latency, and open alerts at a glance. In app monitoring (Observability) Metrics — request volume, success rate, average and p95 latency, token usage ( /metrics/overview , /metrics/timeseries , /metrics/by model ). Cost — spend by model and over time ( /costs/summary ). System health — database, Redis, and per model health ( /system/health ). SLA targets — define thresholds (e.g. success rate ≥ 95%) and evaluate them ( /sla targets/evaluate ). Alert rules — raise alerts when a metric crosses a threshold ( /alert rules ); they appear in Governance alerts. Scheduled checks A Celery beat scheduler runs periodic jobs: model health checks, SLA/alert rule evaluation, webhook delivery, and scheduled workflows. External monitoring A Prometheus metrics endpoint is exposed at the root /metrics for scraping into your existing monitoring stack. The Dashboard surfaces the most important of these (AI Health, cost, p95 latency, open alerts) for at a glance oversight. :::

title: Troubleshooting
description: Common symptoms and how to fix them.
text: Troubleshooting Symptom Likely cause Fix API won't start: "AES 256 needs exactly 32 bytes" Bad MASTER ENCRYPTION KEY Generate a valid 32 byte base64 key Login returns 422 / email rejected Reserved TLD email ( .local / .test ) Use a normal domain (e.g. admin@veevra.io ) A model can't be used Model disabled or no credential Add a credential, then Enable the model Inference unexpectedly blocked A guardrail matched (injection/PII) Check Governance → Violations ; adjust the guardrail Knowledge ingest fails No enabled embedding model Enable an embedding modality model and bind the collection to it Routing picks the wrong model Rule priority / conditions Use Routing → Simulate to see which rule fired; reorder priorities Webhook never arrives Bad URL / non 2xx / signature mismatch Check Webhooks → Deliveries ; verify HMAC and return 2xx quickly Portal can't reach API from another device DHCP changed host IP / firewall Update .env URLs to the host LAN IP; allow ports 3000/8000 Browser shows stale UI after a change (dev) File watch not firing Restart the web container (polling is enabled by default) Health checks GET /health (liveness), GET /health/ready (dependencies). System Health screen: database, Redis, and per model status. Capture the request trace id from the failing call and open it under Requests — the stage timeline shows exactly where it failed or was blocked. ::: For deeper runbooks see docs/RUNBOOK.md in the repository.

title: Implementation Status
description: What is fully implemented today, per module, across backend and frontend.
text: Implementation Status This page reflects the platform as built and running . "✅ Done" means implemented end to end and exercised. "🟡 Partial" means working but with a documented limitation. "⛔ Stub" means a placeholder that validates but does not yet perform the real action. The Product Roadmap lists what's planned. ::: Module status (backend + frontend) Module Backend Frontend Notes IAM & Multi tenancy ✅ ✅ JWT in httpOnly cookies, rotating refresh, TOTP MFA, RBAC (30+ perms), scoped API keys, hash chained audit, SSO/OIDC + group→role mapping Model Registry & Adapters ✅ ✅ 5 real adapters: OpenAI, Anthropic, Azure OpenAI, on prem, generic; encrypted credentials; health & pricing AI Service Bus ✅ ✅ Full 9 stage pipeline, inference, requests/traces, replay, live WebSocket feed Routing & Policy Engine ✅ ✅ Rules router is the default; conditions/actions, fallback chains, A/B experiments, simulate Prompt Manager ✅ ✅ Templates, versions, approval/publish workflow, safe variable rendering, live test Policy & Governance ✅ ✅ PII / national ID / passport / financial masking, injection & jailbreak blocking, content filtering — enabled by default Knowledge / RAG ✅ ✅ pgvector embeddings, chunking, ingestion, semantic search, retrieval grounding with sources AI Agents & Workflows ✅ ✅ Tools, agent runs, memory, multi step workflows, human in the loop approvals, scheduler Observability ✅ ✅ Metrics, cost, SLA targets, alert rules, system health, Prometheus endpoint Integrations & Webhooks 🟡 ✅ Webhooks (HMAC signed, retried) and the HTTP connector are real; email/SMS/e sign/CRM/ERP connectors validate config only (see below) Seed / Config Packages ✅ ✅ 20 declarative packages, idempotent install, export/import, demo tenant Dashboards & System Config ✅ ✅ Configurable widget dashboards; languages/regions/notifications/settings/departments Documentation Center ✅ 🟡 Full docs engine (search, TOC, callouts, Mermaid, screenshots); content ~13 of ~50 planned pages authored Tests ✅ — 25 backend test files (unit + integration) across all modules Deployment ✅ ✅ Docker Compose (dev) + prod compose + Helm chart + CI pipeline Verified limitations (working, but partial) Area Status Detail Streaming ( /inference/stream ) 🟡 Returns the completed answer chunked word by word over SSE — not yet true token by token from the model (adapter stream() seam reserved). Integration connectors ⛔ stub HTTP connector performs a real request; email/SMS/e sign/CRM/ERP only validate config — they do not actually send/sign yet. Vector backend 🟡 pgvector is fully implemented; Qdrant is behind the interface but raises if selected. Knowledge ingestion 🟡 Synchronous/in process (fine at current scale); no async worker queue yet. Specialised models (OCR, speech to text, translation) 🟡 Registered and routable; wired via the generic HTTP adapter — no native engine adapters yet. Agent loop 🟡 Model agnostic JSON protocol loop (works with any model); not native provider function calling. Each turn is one governed inference (observable but heavier). Response caching ⛔ Redis powers rate limiting, the live feed, and the job queue; identical prompt response caching is not implemented. Metrics 🟡 Computed live from inference requests ; no pre aggregated rollup tables (fine at current scale). Governance pre check timing 🟡 Scans raw input + template variable values; it runs before prompt render, so it doesn't see the fully rendered template body (output post check covers the result). require approval guardrail action 🟡 Currently treated as block ; agent/workflow human in the loop approvals are fully implemented separately. SSO / OIDC ✅ Fully implemented (discovery, PKCE, JWKS verification); exercise end to end against a real identity provider as part of go live. All core modules are feature complete and running . The pending items are mostly connector implementations (real email/SMS/CRM sending), performance enhancements (true streaming, caching, async ingestion, metric rollups), and documentation content — none of which block the platform's governed AI workflows today. :::

title: Product Roadmap
description: What is planned next — frontend and backend — grouped by horizon.
text: Product Roadmap All core capabilities are implemented (see Implementation Status). The roadmap below is enhancement and completion work, tagged [BE] backend / [FE] frontend. ::: Near term (completion & polish) Item Area What Finish Documentation Center content [FE] Author the remaining ~35 pages to fully match the docs navigation (content only — no code changes). Ask Veevra assistant [FE+BE] Wire the header assistant to real answers (it is a styled scaffold today) — answer "what needs attention?" from live telemetry. Remaining create wizards [FE] Convert Workflow / Guardrail / Tool / Integration create forms to multi step wizards (Agent, Model, Routing Rule, Provider already done). Real connector delivery [BE] Implement actual sending for email (SMTP), SMS, e sign, CRM, ERP connectors (currently config validation stubs). DataTable on remaining lists [FE] Apply the data grid pattern to the last few list screens. Mid term (performance & depth) Item Area What True token by token streaming [BE+FE] Stream tokens directly from the provider via the adapter stream() seam; live typing in the playground. Async ingestion pipeline [BE] Move knowledge ingestion to Celery workers for large document sets. Response caching [BE] Cache identical governed prompts in Redis to cut cost and latency. Native function calling agents [BE] Optional native provider tool use (alongside the model agnostic JSON loop). require approval → live HITL [BE+FE] Make the guardrail approval action raise a human in the loop task instead of blocking. SSO go live hardening [BE] Exercise OIDC end to end against the customer's real identity provider. Long term (scale & specialised AI) Item Area What Qdrant vector backend [BE] Add Qdrant behind the existing vector store interface for very large knowledge bases. Native specialised adapters [BE] First class OCR, speech to text, and translation adapters (today via the generic HTTP adapter). Metric rollup tables [BE] Pre aggregate metrics for high volume deployments. Advanced analytics & reporting [FE+BE] Deeper cost/usage analytics, exportable compliance reports. Companion app theming (D:\IAM) [FE] Finish the separate IAM Vue app's dark theme parity and QA. None of the above blocks production use of governed AI today. Prioritise near term connector work for customers who need email/SMS/CRM actions, and streaming/caching for high volume, latency sensitive deployments. :::
